channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Trump Hasn’t Given Up on Greenland
item.description: And America’s NATO allies are still on edge about it.

item.title: Israeli Minister’s Taunting of Detained Flotilla Activists Sparks Foreign Backlash
item.description: Foreign leaders and Israeli officials are condemning Itamar Ben-Gvir’s treatment of Global Sumud Flotilla activists.

item.title: Georgia’s Dogs Are Disappearing
item.description: A botched government program has become a PR disaster.

item.title: No, Russia’s Economy Is Not About to Collapse
item.description: The war with Ukraine has slowed growth, but Moscow remains stable.

item.title: Donald Trump’s War on Tourism
item.description: Turned off by U.S. policies and border practices, foreign visitors are going elsewhere.

item.title: Is Trump’s Iran War a Crime of Aggression?
item.description: America’s war on Iran is widely considered an illegal act of aggression in clear violation of the United Nations Charter. But assessing whether the war's planners should face prosecution raises difficult questions, largely owing to ongoing uncertainties about the meaning and scope of criminal aggression.

item.title: Essential India newsletter: Introducing Ashoka, our new column
item.description: Leo Mirani explains how the column got its name

item.title: The Calls for a U.S. War on Cuba Are Getting Louder
item.description: Florida Republicans want Trump to seize indicted former Cuban President Raúl Castro.

item.title: The Putin-Xi Meeting Made the Trump-Xi Summit Look Hollow
item.description: One summit was all symbolism; the other was all substance. China made it clear who its true friend is.

item.title: The Coming Vote for Europe’s Soul
item.description: The principle of non-discrimination between trading partners has underpinned international commerce for eight decades. Now, the same principle must guide European lawmakers as they decide whether to ratify the trade agreement reached last year between European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and US President Donald Trump.

item.title: South Korea’s Conservatives Are Running Out of Time
item.description: Adrift after Yoon’s impeachment, the PPP is facing the unthinkable: loss of support in its long-time base.

item.title: India’s Multialignment Test: Everything, Everywhere All at Once
item.description: The convergence of multiple theaters, crises, and strategic conversations at once makes this perhaps the most consequential test of India’s multialignment strategy.

item.title: Why Are China’s Young People Fed Up?
item.description: Decades of misguided economic policymaking have left China in a demographic hole that it seems incapable of climbing out of. As the “world’s factory,” the country is churning out everything except the people whom it will need to sustain its economic development and social stability over the long term.

item.title: Why Rafale ICD Access Is Critical for India’s Defense Ambitions
item.description: India’s push for domestic defense production is running up against suppliers’ reluctance to transfer technology and key data.

item.title: An Old Turn in Kazakhstan’s Newest Party?
item.description: Rumors link Dariga Nazarbayeva to Adilet.

item.title: Chinese Experts Read the Trump-Xi Summit as a Power-Shift Moment
item.description: A survey of 50 Chinese-language commentaries published around the summit shows the trip being described as a paradigmatic shift.

item.title: Lessons from the Premier League for Britain’s next premier
item.description: How a deflated country can bounce back

item.title: How to stop the Ebola outbreak
item.description: The latest epidemic in central Africa is a warning about future pandemics

item.title: Overseas Chinese risk losing their oldest institutions
item.description: Clan associations must change to survive

item.title: Why Japan and China will struggle to end their feud
item.description: Warming relations between America and China bode ill for Japan

item.title: What China can learn from Japan about escaping deflation
item.description: The right kind of Japanification

item.title: Home-schooling is on the rise around the world
item.description: In the wake of the pandemic, more parents are home-schooling their children

item.title: Leftist populism’s next big test
item.description: In Maine, Graham Platner is betting centrist and right-leaning voters want economic revolution more than culture war

item.title: Europe’s first known language is alive in America’s West
item.description: A corner of Idaho will forever be north-east Spain

item.title: Democratic primary voters chose a dicey candidate for Georgia governor
item.description: Keisha Lance Bottoms will have to run against her record

item.title: America’s sermons are becoming op-eds
item.description: An analysis of nearly 90,000 evangelical sermons quantifies how many pastors are endorsing parties

item.title: Business
The weekly cartoon
Politics
Could microscopic spheres of silica help cool the planet?
item.description: Private money is bringing new ideas—and new concerns—to solar-geoengineering research

item.title: Bre-entry may be the next drama to grip the European Union
item.description: An old friend comes knocking

item.title: How Europe is fighting for digital sovereignty
item.description: Escaping its dependence on America’s tech giants is a tall order

item.title: Real Madrid’s boss calls an election
item.description: Europe’s once-dominant club will put its leadership to its members

item.title: The benefits—and dangers—of optimism
item.description: Why you should (almost) always look on the bright side of life

item.title: Can an Italian company disrupt Germany’s broken railway industry?
item.description: Deutsche Bahn could soon have a rival

item.title: A new mega-deal shows how AI has turned utilities into hot property
item.description: NextEra and Dominion must now win over regulators

item.title: How Star Wars went from space opera to soap opera
item.description: Disney turned its flagship film franchise into TV fodder. Can it go back to the big screen?

item.title: The strange fate of Hard Rock Cafe
item.description: A Native American tribe is making big bucks from the brand

item.title: Donald Trump is still looking for a quick fix in Iran
item.description: But he has delayed another round of military strikes, at least for now

item.title: The mother of the world v the upstart
item.description: Why Egypt needs and fears the United Arab Emirates

item.title: Israel’s economy is booming
item.description: Its endless wars help explain why

item.title: Why Brazil’s government is obsessed with vaccines
item.description: It is spending heavily to revive the country’s medical-industrial complex

item.title: Months after electing a centrist president, Bolivia boils over
item.description: Rodrigo Paz inherited a broken economy. It is getting harder and harder to fix

item.title: SpaceX has initiated the biggest ever public offering
item.description: Elon Musk is again going all-in on an unproven technology—data centres in space

item.title: India’s diplomats are hosting the world
item.description: But what is getting done?

item.title: SpaceX is capitalism on rocket fuel
item.description: Make what you will of Elon Musk, his rocketry firm is a marvel of free markets

item.title: The Myth of the Deserving Billionaire
item.description: While 55% of Americans say their financial situation is deteriorating, the wealth of the top 1% is reaching record highs. Against this backdrop, defending billionaires from “oppressive” taxation is politically convenient nonsense.

item.title: How should economists treat morality?
item.description: Sometimes it is more than merely an exogenous constraint

item.title: The other China shock
item.description: Does the country’s manufacturing success leave space for anyone else?

item.title: The insurers on the hook for war in Iran
item.description: Some have been hammered; even those who haven’t might be soon

item.title: American growth could be even better
item.description: MAGAnomics shows the world what not to do. But also what America keeps getting right

item.title: Why NATO needs a Plan B
item.description: Mark Rutte is wrong to quash talk of one. The risks of the alliance unravelling are too great to ignore

item.title: Drained by war with Iran, America is stalling deliveries of arms to Europe
item.description: Many allies are starting to look for different suppliers

item.title: How Mizo Rebels Hoodwinked Indian and Pakistani Intelligence Agencies
item.description: In his autobiography, Zoramthanga details how MNF rebels procured multiple Pakistani passports and how they escaped from a safehouse in New Delhi

item.title: BJP Victory in West Bengal Intensifies ‘Push-in’ Anxieties in Bangladesh
item.description: Over 80 percent of India’s border with Bangladesh now falls under the direct rule of the BJP, which thrives on anti-migrant rhetoric.

item.title: Myanmar’s National Unity Government: Winning the Moral Argument, but Running Out of Time
item.description: The region is slowly but surely moving toward normalization of the new-look military government.

item.title: What the Indonesia Investment Authority Did in 2025
item.description: When it was founded in 2021, it was not clear exactly what the purpose of the state investment fund was. Last year, the picture started to become more clear

item.title: The Crumbling Pillars of Global Peace
item.description: War, empire, and the forgotten power of the United Nations.

item.title: Philippine Supreme Court Rejects Fugitive Senator’s Challenge to ICC Arrest Warrant
item.description: The decision opens the way for the arrest for the senator, who is wanted by the ICC for his role in leading former President Rodrigo Duterte's violence anti-drug campaign.

item.title: Indonesian President Announces Plan to Centralize Control of Key Commodity Exports
item.description: The move is likely to unsettle international investors already concerned about the uncertain trajectory of economic policymaking under Prabowo.

item.title: Is 2026 the Year of Booming Central Asia-Africa Relations?
item.description: There has been a curious uptick in Central Asia-Africa relations this year, led by Kyrgyzstan.

item.title: Kazakhstan’s Turkic Relations Between Trade, Culture, and Defense
item.description: During last week’s visit by multiple leaders of Turkic states to Kazakhstan, President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev celebrated their shared cultural heritage but remained reluctant on the expansion of defense cooperation.

item.title: What Actually Caused the Latest Submarine Cut Near Taiwan?
item.description: A cable linking Matsu Island to Taiwan was severed – again. And again, Chinese vessels were operating in the area.
